text: The surgeon traces the jejunum proximally to the ligament of Treitz. Using DaVinci Firefly fluorescence imaging, the colonic vasculature is illuminated. A vessel sealer is employed to enter the transverse mesocolon under fluoroscopic guidance.

The surgeon identifies the third portion of the duodenum and partially mobilizes it. A tension-free segment of the proximal jejunum is positioned adjacent to the third portion of the duodenum for anastomosis. A 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to construct the back wall of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses an XI permanent pottery hook to create incisions in the anti-mesenteric wall of the jejunum and duodenum. A SureForm 60mm XI reloadable stapler is then positioned, with its thick firing arm in the jejunotomy and the thin arm in the duodenotomy, to secure the duodenal jejunal anastomosis. The stapler is clamped and fired, ensuring no redundancy in the small bowel tissue.

The surgeon closes the duodenal jejunostomy and oversews the stapled anastomosis using 3-0 V-lock sutures. The mesenteric defect is then closed with a 2-0 V-lock suture to prevent potential herniation of the small bowel.

The surgeon sprays 5 milliliters of Tis-Seal fibrin sealant onto the anastomosis to secure it. The anastomosis is then irrigated with normal saline, followed by an endoscopic leak test, which shows no bubbles, indicating a negative result for leaks.
